frameworks.name: Equity Decomposition Framework (Cash Flow, Sale, Risk, Control)
frameworks.definition: A four-part breakdown of what equity actually consists of (cash flow, sale upside, risk, and control) used to identify which components an employee actually wants so non-dilutive alternatives can be offered in their place.

frameworks.name: OTE Reverse-Engineering for Profit Share
frameworks.definition: A compensation design process that starts from desired on-target earnings for a role, then back-calculates the profit-share percentage required to hit that OTE at expected profit levels, rather than picking an arbitrary percentage.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that at approximately $5 million in revenue, a business is not yet at the scale where true C-suite titles (COO, CMO) are appropriate; the correct title level is VP or director, and using inflated C-suite titles artificially inflates expected compensation without matching the actual scope of the role.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Title signals expected seniority and scope; overtitiing creates compensation expectations the business cannot sustain at that revenue level.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that for an operations leadership hire at the ~$5M revenue stage, LinkedIn targeted outreach to people currently in equivalent roles at similarly-sized auto businesses (or comparable trades) combined with Indeed ads will surface the most viable candidate pool; direct outreach framed as replicating their current role at a new company is the most effective pitch.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the ideal first operations hire for a founder-bottlenecked service business is a people-leader who can absorb escalations, not a systems-and-processes person; what the founder needs relief from is decision escalation, and a character-driven leader can lead almost any operational domain regardless of industry-specific experience.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: leader, not someone who hates talking to people
claims.mechanism: Good operators work two levels above the task (fixing people, not cars); domain knowledge is secondary to the leadership trait of absorbing team problems.
bounded_by: does not apply when the bottleneck is a technical skill gap rather than a leadership gap

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends structuring a profit-share pool of 10-20% of profit distributed among leadership so that as the business grows, the percentage stays fixed and leaders experience growing absolute compensation, creating owner-like incentives without transferring equity.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: When leaders receive a fixed percentage of a growing profit pool, their interests align with expanding total profit rather than protecting personal allocation, replicating equity incentives without dilution.

numbers.axis: profit share pool as percentage of total profit

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when employees say they want equity, they typically only actually want two of its four components (cash flow and sale upside); the other two components (risk and control) are unwanted. This realization enables founders to offer phantom equity structures that deliver the wanted components without transferring control or adding shared risk.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Decomposing equity into four named components (cash flow, sale upside, risk, control) exposes that most employees do not actually want risk or control, allowing founders to satisfy the stated desire with non-dilutive instruments.
bounded_by: does not apply to co-founders or early investors who seek control explicitly

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends a phantom equity structure for key operations hires in which the employee earns 1% of profits interest per year of tenure (up to a defined cap, e.g., 4%), the interest vests only while employed (reverts on departure), and the full vested percentage pays out as a sale bonus only if the employee is present at the time of sale and has been employed for at least 12-24 months. The tax consequence is ordinary income rates on the sale bonus rather than capital gains.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder seeks to retain key operator without diluting cap table
claims.mechanism: Phantom structure replicates the sale-upside component of equity (the component employees actually want) while keeping legal ownership entirely with the founder; clawback on departure prevents free-rider problem.

numbers.axis: phantom equity accrual rate per year of tenure

bounded_by: employee pays ordinary income tax on sale bonus, not capital-gains rates; employees preferring capital-gains treatment must instead take actual equity and pay taxes on grant-date value

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in a geographically constrained (local/regional) service business, a growth-increment percentage bonus (e.g., 30% of year-over-year growth) is a poor incentive structure because local market capacity limits prevent reliable growth, discouraging and demoralizing the operator. A straight profit-share percentage of total profit is preferred for these businesses.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: non-geographically constrained fast-growth businesses are the comparison
claims.mechanism: Local market capacity caps growth potential; tying compensation to growth rate punishes operators for market constraints outside their control, reducing motivation.

numbers.axis: growth-incentive percentage for non-geographically constrained businesses

anchors.region: example growth bonus for fast-growth unconstrained business

bounded_by: growth-increment bonus is appropriate for businesses not geographically constrained

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends reverse-engineering the profit-share percentage from the desired on-target earnings (OTE) for the role rather than selecting an arbitrary percentage. The specific percentage is irrelevant; what matters is that three compensation scenarios are defined: below target (termination risk), on target, and above target (upside).
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: profit-share compensation design for any role
claims.mechanism: Starting from desired OTE and working backward to percentage aligns compensation structure to role market rate rather than to an arbitrary number that may be either too dilutive or too low to motivate.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for private multi-location brick-and-mortar expansion, having a profit-share-aligned operator boots-on-the-ground at each location is the necessary condition for genuine decentralization and independent unit operation; without a profit-tied operator at each location, expansion stalls under founder hand-holding.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder seeks to expand without proportional time increase
claims.mechanism: Profit-share alignment gives the location operator incentives to make decisions autonomously, because their compensation rises or falls with location profit, removing the need to escalate to the founder.
bounded_by: Hormozi has not yet observed a private operator do exceptional multi-location expansion without a profit-tied boots-on-ground person at each store

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends running separate P&Ls per location to enable location-level profit-share calculations, even within a single-entity legal structure, as long as the financials can be calculated at the location level.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: multi-location business without separate LLCs per location

examples.text: During the conversation, Hormozi illustrated the OTE reverse-engineering method using an auto hail repair business with four locations, where one location generates approximately $200,000 per month in profit. Using that location as the reference: at a 10% profit share, the operator would receive $5,000/month extra ($60,000/year), bringing total comp to approximately $160,000 if base salary is $100,000. Hormozi noted that at this business's stage (approximately $50,000/month per-location profit when averaged across all four stores), whether 5% or 10% is chosen is less important than reverse-engineering the percentage to match the desired OTE for the role.

Hormozi illustrated the equity decomposition framework by walking through a hypothetical conversation with an employee who says they want equity. He would ask: do you want risk? (No.) Do you want control? (No, and I won't give it.) That leaves cash flow and sale upside, both of which can be replicated via a profit-share pool (for cash flow) and a phantom equity interest that pays out on sale (for exit upside). The employee gets both desired components without receiving actual equity, protecting founder control.

practices.text: When seeking a first operations hire at the ~$5M revenue stage, offer a relocation package or signing bonus to attract candidates from outside the local area if the local talent pool is insufficient.
When designing profit-share structures, set explicit three-tier performance lines: below target (termination risk), on target (OTE), and above target (upside cap) before presenting the offer.

For phantom equity grants, include a minimum tenure requirement (12-24 months present at time of sale) and a clawback clause (interest reverts to zero on voluntary departure) before presenting the structure to a prospective hire.
